worth_it_explanation: Awareness and reach goals are easy to misread when they are judged by the wrong number. A campaign measured on clicks reports almost nothing for an objective that clicks were never meant to capture, and a reach figure read in impressions counts exposures rather than people. These checks confirm the campaign is measured on the outcome it is meant to drive, which changes how its results are read rather than how it is built.
intro: Awareness and upper-funnel goals on Amazon DSP are measured by lift and by reach, not by clicks. Brand lift studies test whether exposure moved awareness or consideration, and unique reach and frequency count how many people saw the ads and how often. These checks confirm that awareness line items are measured on lift rather than clicks, that reach is read as people rather than raw impressions, and that the figures are reconciled against third-party measurement where it runs.

audit_checks.title: Awareness line items are judged by clicks instead of brand lift
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An awareness campaign measured by clicks reports a low click-through rate that says nothing about whether awareness moved. The line item looks like a failure on a metric it was never meant to drive, and a working upper-funnel tactic can be cut on that misread.

educate.base:
Brand lift measurement tests whether ad exposure changed an awareness or consideration outcome, by comparing a group that saw the ads against a comparable group that did not. It is the measurement built for upper-funnel goals, where the action the campaign wants is a shift in perception rather than an immediate click or purchase.

When an awareness line item is judged by click-through rate instead, the number reports almost nothing useful. Display and streaming TV (STV) impressions rarely drive clicks, so a low click-through rate is expected and says nothing about whether awareness rose. Reading the line item on that metric makes a working tactic look like a failure.

A few signs point to an awareness goal measured on the wrong metric:
  • An upper-funnel line item is reported on click-through rate or cost per click with no lift measurement
  • No brand lift study is set up for a campaign whose stated goal is awareness or consideration
  • Budget decisions on awareness line items are being made from click metrics
You may want to confirm that a brand lift study, or another awareness measure, is in place before reading an upper-funnel line item's results. A clicks-based read of an awareness goal understates it.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a seller or vendor advertiser, an awareness line item often supports later branded search and detail-page traffic rather than driving an immediate purchase. You may want to measure it with brand lift or new-to-brand rather than the click metrics that suit a lower-funnel line item.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a non-endemic advertiser, an upper-funnel streaming TV or display line item is meant to build recall before the off-Amazon conversion. You may want to confirm a lift measure is in place rather than judging it on clicks the format does not produce.

investigate.intro: Identify the awareness line items and confirm whether a lift measure is in place or whether they are reported on clicks.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: List the line items whose stated goal is awareness or consideration.
Check whether a brand lift study or another awareness measure is attached to them.
Note any awareness line item being reported and optimized on click-through rate or cost per click alone.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per awareness line item, whether a lift measure is in place and which metric is used to judge it. An awareness line item measured by lift passes. An awareness line item judged by clicks with no lift measure is a fail recorded with the line item.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the awareness line items and their measurement method above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s awareness line items on Amazon DSP are judged by click metrics rather than brand lift. Display and streaming TV impressions rarely drive clicks, so the reported click-through rate says nothing about whether awareness moved, and a working upper-funnel tactic can be cut on that misread. {{voice_recommend}} setting up a brand lift study for the awareness campaigns and reading those line items on the lift result rather than on clicks.

audit_checks.title: Reach is read in impressions rather than unique people
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Impressions count exposures, not people, so a campaign that served many impressions to a small audience reads as wide reach when it is actually high frequency. Budget meant to extend reach is spent re-hitting the same users.

educate.base:
Reach is the count of unique people who saw the ads, and frequency is how often each person saw them. Impressions are the product of the two, so an impression total alone cannot tell whether a campaign reached many people once or few people many times.

Reading reach in impressions hides that difference. A line item that served a large impression total to a narrow audience reports a big number that looks like broad reach, when the audience was small and the frequency high. For an awareness goal that depends on reaching new people, this matters since budget meant to extend reach is spent re-hitting users already exposed.

A few signs point to reach being read the wrong way:
  • Reporting cites impressions as the reach figure with no unique-reach number alongside
  • An awareness line item shows a high impression total against a small targeted audience
  • Average frequency is high while the campaign goal is to reach new people
You may want to read unique reach and average frequency together, and consider whether a high frequency against a small audience is serving the goal or just spending against the same people.

site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a non-endemic advertiser running an upper-funnel campaign, unique reach is the figure that shows how many new prospects the budget actually touched. You may want to confirm reporting cites people reached rather than impressions served.

investigate.intro: Check whether reporting cites unique reach and frequency or only impressions, and read them together for awareness line items.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Pull the unique reach and average frequency for the awareness line items.
Check whether the team reports reach as people or as impressions.
Flag any line item with a high impression total, a small audience, and a high average frequency.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records unique reach, average frequency, and impressions for the awareness line items, and how reach is reported. A line item read on unique reach with a reasonable frequency passes. A line item reported on impressions, or showing high frequency against a small audience, is a fail recorded with the line item.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record unique reach, frequency, and how reach is reported above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that reach on {{site_name}}'s awareness line items is read in impressions rather than unique people. An impression total hides whether the campaign reached many people once or few people many times, so a small audience served at high frequency can read as broad reach. {{voice_recommend}} reporting unique reach and average frequency together for the awareness line items, and adjusting frequency caps where the budget is re-hitting the same users rather than extending reach.

audit_checks.title: Amazon reach and lift figures are not reconciled against third-party measurement
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When a third-party measurement study runs alongside the platform numbers and the two are never compared, a discrepancy that should prompt investigation goes unnoticed. The advertiser trusts one number without knowing the other disagrees.

educate.base:
Advertisers often run a third-party measurement study, for example a verification vendor's reach and frequency measurement or an independent brand lift study, alongside the figures Amazon DSP reports. The two are built differently and rarely match exactly, so the value comes from comparing them and understanding why they differ.

When the platform numbers and the third-party study are filed separately and never reconciled, a meaningful gap goes unnoticed. A large difference in measured reach or in lift can point to a counting difference, a tagging problem, or a real measurement issue, and none of that surfaces if nobody compares the two.

A few signs point to measurement that is not reconciled:
  • A third-party study runs but its results are never compared against the Amazon DSP figures
  • A known discrepancy between the two has no explanation on record
  • Reporting cites one source without acknowledging the other ran
You may want to compare the platform and third-party figures for the same campaign and window, and treat a large unexplained gap as something to trace rather than to average away.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a seller or vendor advertiser running a third-party reach study, the comparison against the Amazon DSP figures can reveal whether co-viewing on streaming TV or a tagging gap explains a difference. You may want to reconcile the two for the same campaign rather than read them in isolation.

investigate.intro: Check whether a third-party measurement study runs alongside the platform figures and whether the two have been compared.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Identify any third-party reach or brand lift study running on the campaign.
Pull the matching Amazon DSP figures for the same campaign and window.
Compare the two and note any large gap that has no explanation on record.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a third-party study runs and whether its results were compared against the Amazon DSP figures. A campaign whose two sources are reconciled, with any gap explained, passes. A campaign with an unreconciled study, or an unexplained gap, is a fail recorded with the metric and the size of the difference.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the third-party study, the Amazon DSP figures, and any gap above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs third-party measurement alongside Amazon DSP but does not reconcile the two. The platform and third-party figures are built differently and rarely match, so a gap that should prompt investigation goes unnoticed when nobody compares them. {{voice_recommend}} comparing the reach and lift figures for the same campaign and window, and tracing any large difference to its cause rather than reporting one source as if the other had agreed.
